When muscle contraction is initiated, tropomyosin shifts to expose these sites, allowing myosin to bind to actin. Myosin itself is responsible for the contraction by forming cross-bridges with actin but does not wrap around it. Troponin regulates the position of tropomyosin by responding to calcium ion concentrations, but it is not the protein that twists around actin. Creatine is unrelated to the structural dynamics between actin and tropomyosin, as it functions mainly in energy storage and supply in muscle cells.

questions.text: It remains fixed during muscle contraction:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Correct. The origin is the fixed attachment point of a muscle to a bone, and it does not move during muscle contraction. It serves as the anchor from which the muscle exerts force.
Incorrect. The insertion is the attachment point of a muscle to a more mobile bone, and it moves when the muscle contracts, contributing to movement.
Incorrect. The belly is the thicker, middle part of the muscle, which actively shortens and thickens during contraction, therefore not remaining fixed.
Incorrect. Bones can serve as levers in muscle contraction and may move when muscles pull on them, so they do not remain fixed.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Origin. During muscle contraction, the origin serves as the fixed attachment point, remaining stationary while the muscle exerts force to move other parts of the body. The Insertion point, however, is where the muscle attaches to the bone that moves, allowing for motion. The Belly of the muscle is the central part that contracts and changes shape, hence not fixed. Lastly, Bones themselves can move when muscles contract, especially those acting as levers, so they are not fixed during contraction.

questions.text: _can polarize visible light:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect. The M-line is located at the center of the sarcomere and helps stabilize the thick filaments. It does not have the structural characteristics required to polarize visible light.
Incorrect. The I-band contains only thin filaments and appears lighter in unstained sarcomeres. It lacks the filament overlap necessary to polarize visible light.
Incorrect. The H-band of the sarcomere is located at the center of the A-band. The H-band contains only thick filaments and is not involved in polarizing visible light.
Correct. The A-band contains overlapping thick and thin filaments. This overlap allows it to polarize visible light, contributing to its darker appearance under polarized microscopy.

questions.explanation: The A-band is unique in its ability to polarize visible light due to the overlap of thick (myosin) and thin (actin) filaments. This structural characteristic causes the A-band to appear darker when viewed under polarized light, a property not shared by the M-line, I-band, or H-band. The M-line stabilizes thick filaments, the I-band includes only thin filaments, and the H-band encompasses the central region of thick filaments, none of which contribute to light polarization.

questions.text: It is the length of myofibril from one Z-line to the next:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect. The plasma membrane refers to the outer membrane of the cell, not the structure within the myofibril.
Correct. A sarcomere is the segment of a myofibril between two Z-lines and is the functional unit of muscle contraction.
Incorrect. The sarcoplasm refers to the cytoplasm of a muscle cell, which encompasses the components necessary for cellular metabolism, but not the contractile units.
Incorrect. The sarcolemma is the membrane that surrounds the muscle cell, playing a role in conducting electrical signals, not in defining the contractile units.

questions.explanation: The sarcomere is the length of a myofibril from one Z-line to the next and is the basic contractile unit within muscle cells. During muscle contraction, the sarcomeres shorten as the actin and myosin filaments slide past one another, bringing Z-lines closer together. The other options, such as the plasma membrane, sarcoplasm, and sarcolemma, refer to different components of the muscle cell that do not define this specific structural unit.

questions.text: Muscle cell is considered as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Correct. A muscle fiber is a single muscle cell that acts as the building block of muscle tissue, enabling movement through contraction.
Incorrect. A sarcomere is a segment within a muscle fiber responsible for contraction, composed of actin and myosin, but not a complete muscle cell itself.
Incorrect. A muscle bundle consists of multiple muscle fibers grouped together, surrounded by connective tissue, rather than being a single muscle cell.
Incorrect. Myofibrils are thread-like structures inside a muscle fiber that facilitate contraction, but they do not constitute an entire muscle cell.

questions.explanation: Each muscle cell is referred to as a muscle fiber, providing the basic functional unit of muscle tissue. While sarcomeres and myofibrils are critical components within a muscle fiber, they are not standalone muscle cells. Muscle bundles, on the other hand, are collections of muscle fibers, not individual cells.

questions.text: Smooth endoplasmic reticulum is similar in structure to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect. The Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum (RER) is distinguished by the presence of ribosomes on its surface, playing a pivotal role in protein synthesis, which is not directly involved in muscle contraction.
Incorrect. The Golgi apparatus is involved in modifying, sorting, and packaging proteins and lipids for secretion or use within the cell, but it does not play a direct role in muscle contraction.

Incorrect. Microfilaments are part of the cytoskeleton, primarily composed of actin, and are involved in cell movement and structure, not in the storage or regulation of calcium ions like the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

Correct. The sarcoplasmic reticulum is a specialized form of the smooth endoplasmic reticulum found in muscle cells, crucial for storing and releasing calcium ions, which are essential for muscle contraction.

questions.explanation: The sarcoplasmic reticulum is a specialized type of smooth endoplasmic reticulum found in muscle cells. Its main function is to store and release calcium ions, which are critical for initiating and regulating muscle contraction. When a muscle fiber is stimulated, calcium is released from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, binding to troponin and allowing actin and myosin interactions to occur, leading to muscle contraction. The other options: RER, Golgi bodies, and microfilaments do not perform the same function. RER is involved in protein synthesis, Golgi bodies are involved in modifying and packaging proteins, and microfilaments are structural components, none of which directly relate to the function of the sarcoplasmic reticulum in muscle contraction.

questions.text: Pick the ranges of muscle fibre length.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect. This range is too narrow for muscle fibers, which typically have a larger diameter.
Incorrect. This range is too small for muscle fibers, which are generally much larger.
Correct. Muscle fibers can range in diameter from 10 to 100 µm, accommodating their need for contraction and strength.
Incorrect. This option incorrectly uses 'mn' instead of 'µm' for micrometers, and the range is not applicable.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C: 10-100 µm. Muscle fibers are specialized cells that can vary in diameter within this range, allowing them to contract effectively and provide strength. Option A, 5-10 µm, and Option B, 1-2 µm, are both too small for typical muscle fibers. Option D, 50-100 mn, uses an incorrect unit ('mn' instead of 'µm') and is not a valid range for muscle fibers.

questions.text: The thin filaments extend across the I-band and partly into:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect. The Z-line (or Z-disc) is the boundary of a sarcomere and bisects the I-band, but it is not where thin filaments partly extend into. Instead, it anchors the thin filaments of adjacent sarcomeres.
Incorrect. The H-zone is a central region of the A-band where there are no thin filaments, only thick filaments. Thus, thin filaments do not extend into the H-zone.

Correct. The A-band is the region within a sarcomere that contains the entire length of thick myosin filaments. It also includes the overlapping portions where thin actin filaments extend, making it the correct location where thin filaments partly extend.
Incorrect. The M-line is located at the center of the A-band and is where the thick filaments are anchored. It does not involve the extension of thin filaments.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the A-band. The thin filaments extend across the I-band and partly into the A-band, where they overlap with the thick myosin filaments. This overlap is crucial for the sliding filament theory of muscle contraction. The Z-line anchors the thin filaments at the boundary of the sarcomere, while the H-zone is a part of the A-band that contains only thick filaments without thin filaments overlapping. The M-line is the center line of the sarcomere and does not involve thin filaments extending into it.

questions.text: The _ have mid-section called H-zone:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect. The H-zone is actually the region within the A-band where there are only thick filaments, not the entire A-band itself. It is not the mid-section of the sarcomere.
Incorrect. The M-line is the line at the center of the sarcomere that holds the thick filaments together, not the mid-section of the A-band.
Incorrect. The Z-line marks the boundaries of each sarcomere and is not related to the H-zone or the mid-section of the A-band.
Correct. The A-band is the dark band within the sarcomere, which includes the H-zone as its mid-section. It contains thick filaments throughout and overlapping thin filaments at the edges.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D. The A-band is the part of the sarcomere containing thick myosin filaments, and its mid-section is known as the H-zone, where only thick filaments are present. The H-zone itself is not a broad part of the A-band but a specific region within it. The M-line supports the structure of the thick filaments, while the Z-line defines the boundaries of the sarcomere, none of which designate the mid-section of the A-band.

questions.text: Pick a complex of three polypeptide chains protein.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect. Tropomyosin stabilizes actin filaments and regulates the access of actin-binding proteins, but it does not consist of three polypeptide chains.
Incorrect. Actin forms the thin filaments of the muscle sarcomere and is essential for muscle contraction, but it is a single polypeptide chain, not a complex of three.
Incorrect. Myosin is a motor protein that interacts with actin to produce muscle contraction and consists of multiple subunits but not specifically three polypeptide chains.
Correct. Troponin is a protein complex consisting of three polypeptide chains that regulates muscle contraction by controlling the calcium-mediated activation of actin-myosin interactions.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Troponin. Troponin is a complex of three polypeptide chains (Troponin C, Troponin I, and Troponin T) and plays a critical role in muscle contraction by regulating the calcium sensitivity of the sarcomere. It is responsible for initiating the conformational change that allows myosin to bind to actin. On the other hand, Tropomyosin, Actin, and Myosin are essential muscle proteins, but they do not form a complex of three polypeptide chains. Tropomyosin stabilizes actin filaments, Actin forms the thin filaments, and Myosin acts as a molecular motor.

questions.text: After death, the amount_in the body falls:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Water content in the body affects many physiological processes, but it is not directly responsible for muscle stiffness after death.
Calcium plays a crucial role in muscle contraction by enabling the interaction between actin and myosin, but its levels do not necessarily decrease after death.
While oxygen is important for metabolic processes, its depletion is not directly linked to muscle stiffness following death.
After death, the production of ATP halts, leading to a decrease in its availability. Without ATP, myosin heads cannot detach from actin filaments, resulting in muscle stiffness known as rigor mortis.

questions.explanation: After death, ATP production ceases, and its levels fall. ATP is crucial for detaching myosin heads from actin filaments during muscle relaxation. Without sufficient ATP, muscles become rigid, a condition known as rigor mortis. In contrast, water, calcium, and oxygen, while important to various bodily functions, do not specifically cause muscle stiffness after death.

questions.text: Majority of muscles tissue in human body are_type:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect. Smooth muscles are found in internal organs such as the intestines and blood vessels. They are involuntary and do not make up the majority of muscle tissue in the body.
Incorrect. Circular muscles, also known as sphincters, control the opening and closing of bodily passages, like the mouth and anus. They are not a major type of muscle tissue in the body.
Incorrect. Cardiac muscles are found in the heart, responsible for pumping blood. They are involuntary and specialized, not the majority type of muscle in the body.
Correct. Skeletal muscles are attached to bones and are responsible for voluntary movements like walking and lifting. They make up the majority of muscle tissue in the human body.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Skeletal'. Skeletal muscles comprise the majority of muscle tissue in the human body. They are under voluntary control, enabling movements such as walking and lifting. In contrast, smooth muscles are found in organs and are involuntary, cardiac muscles are specific to the heart, and circular muscles control openings in the body. None of these are as abundant as skeletal muscles.

questions.text: Bones protect critical internal organs like
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct option. The skull protects the brain, the vertebral column safeguards the spinal cord, and the ribcage shields the heart.
This option is incorrect. While bones protect the brain and spinal cord, they do not protect all nerves, which are distributed throughout the body and not shielded by bone structures.
This option is incorrect. The stomach is primarily protected by muscles, not bones, and the eyes are protected by eyelids, tears, and eyelashes, rather than bones.

options.text: Spinal cord, Lungs, Pinna of ears
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While the spinal cord is protected by the vertebral column, the pinna of ears is not a critical organ protected by bones, and the lungs, although partially shielded by the rib cage, are primarily protected by the pleura.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that bones protect the brain, spinal cord, and heart. The skull encases the brain, the vertebral column surrounds the spinal cord, and the ribcage protects the heart. Option B is incorrect because all nerves are not protected by bones. Option C is incorrect because bones do not protect the stomach and eyes—these are protected by other structures. Option D is incorrect because the pinna of the ears is not shielded by bone, and while the ribcage helps protect the lungs, they have additional protection from the pleura.

questions.text: A statement that is not true about bones and cartilage
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is true as both bones and cartilage have living cells. Bones have cells such as osteocytes, and cartilage has chondrocytes.

options.text: Both contain various types of living cells
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Cartilage contains only one type of living cell, chondrocytes, while bones have various types, including osteoblasts, osteocytes, and osteoclasts.

options.text: Both have a ground matrix of collagen
options.explanation: This statement is true. Both bones and cartilage have a matrix rich in collagen, providing structural support.

options.text: Both are parts of the endoskeleton
options.explanation: This statement is true as bones and cartilage are components of the vertebrate endoskeleton, providing support and structure to the body.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B: 'Both contain various types of living cells.' This statement is false because cartilage consists of one primary type of cell, chondrocytes, whereas bones contain multiple cell types, including osteoblasts, osteocytes, and osteoclasts. Option A is true since both tissues have living cells. Option C is true because both tissues have collagen in their matrix. Option D is true as both are integral to the endoskeleton.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a function of Bone ?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Bones provide structural support and protect internal organs such as the brain, heart, and spinal cord from external damage.
Bones serve as attachment points for muscles, facilitating movement and providing mechanical leverage.
Bones do not protect against hearing loss. Hearing loss involves issues with the ears or auditory pathways, which are not functions performed by bones.
Bones play a vital role in calcium metabolism by storing and releasing calcium ions, which are essential for various physiological processes.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Protection from hearing loss.' Bones do not protect against hearing loss, which is related to the auditory system. Option A, 'Organ protection,' is a correct function of bones, as they safeguard vital organs. Option B, 'Muscle attachment,' is also a proper function, as bones provide anchorage for muscles to enable movement. Option D, 'Calcium metabolism,' involves bones regulating calcium levels, which is crucial for maintaining bone strength and health.

questions.text: Bone is surrounded by a membrane called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Perichondrium is the membrane that surrounds cartilage, not bone. It aids in the growth and maintenance of cartilage tissues.
Peristomium is not a recognized term related to bone or cartilage membranes. It's a distractor option.
Periosteum is the correct term for the dense fibrous membrane that surrounds bones (except at the joints). It is essential for bone growth, repair, and nutrition.
This option is incorrect because there is a well-known membrane that surrounds bones: the periosteum.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'periosteum.' The periosteum is a dense fibrous membrane that surrounds bones, except at their articular surfaces, where bones meet to form joints. It contains blood vessels, nerves, and connective tissues that are crucial for the growth, repair, and nutrition of bones. The perichondrium, on the other hand, surrounds cartilage, not bone. 'Peristomium' is not a recognized term in this context, and choosing 'None' would be incorrect because such a membrane does exist and is called the periosteum.

questions.text: End of the long bones are covered by
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tendons are fibrous connective tissues that attach muscles to bones, but they don't cover the ends of long bones. Their primary role is to facilitate movement by transmitting forces from muscles to bones.
The ends of long bones are covered by cartilage, specifically articular cartilage, which serves to reduce friction and absorb shock in the joints, allowing for smooth and pain-free movement.
Ligaments are tough bands of connective tissue that connect bone to bone, providing stability to joints. They do not cover the ends of long bones but are essential for joint stability.
Muscles are attached to bones via tendons and are responsible for movement. They do not cover the ends of long bones.

questions.explanation: The ends of long bones are covered by cartilage, specifically articular cartilage. This type of cartilage is crucial for reducing friction and absorbing shock at the joint surfaces, facilitating smooth movement and protecting bone ends. Tendons, ligaments, and muscles have different roles: tendons attach muscles to bones, ligaments stabilize joints by connecting bones, and muscles facilitate movement but do not cover bone ends.

questions.text: The bone dissolving cells are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Osteoclasts are specialized cells tasked with bone resorption. They break down bone tissue by secreting acids and enzymes, which is essential for bone remodeling and calcium regulation.
Osteoblasts are responsible for forming new bone tissue by synthesizing and secreting the bone matrix. They do not dissolve bone; instead, they contribute to its construction.
Osteocytes are mature bone cells embedded within the bone matrix. Their role is to maintain bone tissue, not to dissolve it.
Fibroblasts are cells associated with connective tissue, producing extracellular matrix and collagen. They are not involved in bone resorption.

questions.explanation: Osteoclasts are the bone-dissolving cells responsible for bone resorption. Derived from monocytes, they are large, multinucleated cells that break down bone tissue by secreting enzymes and acids, creating resorption pits. This process is vital for bone remodeling and calcium release into the bloodstream. Osteoblasts then fill these pits with new bone tissue, maintaining bone health.The other cell types serve different functions: Osteoblasts create new bone tissue, osteocytes maintain the bone matrix, and fibroblasts are involved in the production of connective tissue, not bone resorption.

questions.text: The process of bone formation is called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Calcification is the deposition of calcium salts in tissues, which can occur in both bone and non-bone tissues, but it is not the specific process of bone formation.
Decalcification is the removal of calcium salts from tissues, typically used in the context of preparing samples for microscopic examination, and is not related to the formation of bone.
Ossification is the correct term for bone formation. It is the process where new bone is produced from cartilage or fibrous tissue, resulting in the development of hard, mineralized bone tissue.
Chondrification is the process of forming cartilage, which precedes ossification in some bones, but it does not directly result in bone formation.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Ossification. This process involves the transformation of cartilage or fibrous tissue into bone, enabling the development and growth of the skeletal system. Although Calcification involves the deposition of calcium, it occurs in various tissues and is not specific to bone formation. Decalcification refers to the removal of calcium, primarily used in laboratory settings. Chondrification is the formation of cartilage, which can be a precursor to bone formation but is not the process of forming bone itself.

questions.text: The collagen fibers of bones are hardened by deposits of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Correct! Calcium phosphate is crucial for bone strength, forming hydroxyapatite crystals that reinforce the bone matrix.
Incorrect. While calcium carbonate is significant in some biological structures, it is not the main mineral that hardens bone collagen fibers.
Incorrect. Calcium chloride doesn't contribute to bone matrix hardening, as it is a soluble salt and not a structural component.
Incorrect. Although essential in the body, magnesium phosphate does not play a primary role in the hardening of bone collagen fibers.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Calcium phosphate. It is the primary mineral in bones, forming hydroxyapatite crystals that give the bone matrix its strength and rigidity. Other options like calcium carbonate, calcium chloride, and magnesium phosphate are either not involved or not the main contributors to the hardening of bone collagen fibers.

questions.text: The living cell of the cartilage is called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Osteocytes are the cells responsible for maintaining bone tissue, not cartilage. They are embedded in the mineralized matrix of bones.
This is the correct answer. Chondrocytes are the specialized cells found in cartilage that produce and maintain the cartilage matrix, ensuring the tissue's integrity and function.
Fibrocytes are cells that inhabit connective tissues like tendons and ligaments. They are not involved in cartilage tissue.
Chondroblasts are immature cells that develop into chondrocytes during cartilage formation. They actively produce the cartilage matrix before maturing into chondrocytes.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Chondrocytes. Chondrocytes are the specialized cells responsible for producing and maintaining the extracellular matrix of cartilage. They play a crucial role in sustaining the tissue's structure and function. In contrast, Osteocytes are cells found in bone tissue, Fibrocytes are associated with connective tissues like tendons and ligaments, and Chondroblasts are immature cells that mature into chondrocytes during cartilage development.

questions.text: Cartilages heals slowly because
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Osteoclasts are involved in bone resorption, not cartilage healing. The presence or absence of osteoclasts is not a determining factor in the healing rate of cartilage.
This is correct. Cartilage lacks blood vessels, which significantly limits the delivery of nutrients and immune cells to the site of injury, resulting in slower healing.
This is incorrect. Cartilage is rich in collagen, which contributes to its structural integrity. The healing of cartilage is not hindered by the absence of collagen.
This is incorrect. One of the options provided accurately describes the reason for the slow healing of cartilage.

questions.explanation: Cartilage heals slowly primarily because it lacks blood vessels. This absence restricts the supply of essential nutrients and immune cells to the damaged area, which are crucial for the healing process. Consequently, cartilage repair is considerably slower compared to tissues that are well-vascularized. The other options are incorrect because osteoclasts are not involved in cartilage healing, and cartilage contains collagen, contrary to what is suggested in Option C.

questions.text: Cartilage have_ % of inorganic salts
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cartilage does not contain 35% of inorganic salts. It primarily consists of water, collagen, and chondrocytes.
Cartilage does not contain 30% of inorganic salts. It is made up mainly of organic components, not minerals.
Cartilage does not contain 65% of inorganic salts. That percentage is more typical of bones, not cartilage.
Cartilage does not contain inorganic salts. It is a flexible connective tissue mainly composed of water, collagen fibers, and other organic substances.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is None. Cartilage is a flexible connective tissue that does not contain inorganic salts. These salts are characteristic of bones, which rely on them for strength and rigidity. The other options (35%, 30%, 65%) incorrectly associate cartilage with mineral content similar to that of bones.

questions.text: Which cartilage is present at the end of long bones?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fibrous cartilage is tough and provides support and tensile strength, often found in intervertebral discs and pubic symphysis, but it is not present at the ends of long bones.
Hyaline cartilage is smooth and glassy, providing a low-friction surface at the ends of long bones, essential for joint movement and stability.
Elastic cartilage is flexible and resilient, found in structures like the ear and epiglottis, but it does not serve the function at the ends of long bones.
Calcified cartilage is associated with bone development and growth, but it is not the type of cartilage that is present at the ends of long bones.

questions.explanation: Articular cartilage at the ends of long bones is hyaline cartilage, reducing friction in joints.

questions.text: Which of the following cartilage is present in epiglottis
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Elastic cartilage is present in the epiglottis. It is characterized by a network of elastic fibers, allowing it to flex and return to its original shape, which is essential for its function during swallowing.
Fibrous cartilage is more rigid and tough, found in areas subjected to heavy pressure and tension like intervertebral discs and the pubic symphysis, not in the epiglottis.
Hyaline cartilage is the most common type found in joints, providing smooth surfaces for movement, but it does not provide the flexibility needed in the epiglottis.
Stratified cartilage is not a recognized scientific term for a type of cartilage. This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Elastic cartilage is the correct answer because it is found in the epiglottis, where flexibility and resilience are required for its role in covering the windpipe during swallowing. Elastic fibers in this cartilage allow it to bend and quickly return to shape. Fibrous cartilage, on the other hand, is more suitable for high-stress areas like intervertebral discs, while hyaline cartilage provides smooth surfaces for joints. 'Stratified' is not a type of cartilage, making that option invalid.

questions.text: Mature cells of the cartilage is called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chondrocytes are the mature cells found in cartilage tissue. They play a crucial role in maintaining the cartilage by producing and regulating the extracellular matrix.
Osteocytes are mature bone cells embedded within bone tissue, involved in maintaining bone homeostasis, not cartilage.
Osteoclasts are responsible for bone resorption, breaking down bone tissue during remodeling. They are not associated with cartilage.
Osteoblasts are involved in the formation of new bone by secreting bone matrix. They do not pertain to cartilage cell function.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Chondrocytes, which are the mature cells found in cartilage. They are essential for the maintenance and repair of cartilage by regulating the extracellular matrix. In contrast, Osteocytes, Osteoclasts, and Osteoblasts are all related to bone tissue with different roles in bone maintenance, resorption, and formation, respectively.

questions.text: The joints which allows only rotational movements is called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A hinge joint permits movement in one direction, like bending and straightening, similar to a door hinge, and does not allow rotational movement.
A pivot joint enables rotational movement around a single axis, exemplified by the joint between the atlas and axis in the neck, allowing head rotation.
A saddle joint allows movement in two planes, such as flexion-extension and abduction-adduction, providing a wide range of motion as seen in the thumb joint.
'Multistage joint' is not a recognized type of joint. This choice is incorrect as it does not describe any known joint movement.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the pivot joint, which allows only rotational movements around a central axis. An example of this is the joint between the atlas and axis vertebrae, enabling the head to turn from side to side. In contrast, a hinge joint permits movement in only one plane, similar to a door hinge, such as in the elbow or knee. A saddle joint offers a range of movements across two planes and is found in the thumb, allowing more diverse movements. Finally, a 'multistage joint' is not a valid classification in joint anatomy, making it an incorrect option.

questions.text: These joints are held together by short fibers embedded in connective tissues
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Synovial joints have a joint capsule and are not held together by short fibers. They are highly mobile and contain synovial fluid for lubrication.
Saddle joints are a subtype of synovial joints and, like all synovial joints, are not held together by short fibers. They allow movements such as flexion, extension, and circumduction.

Fibrous joints are held together by short fibers embedded in connective tissues, which allow minimal movement. Examples include the sutures in the skull and the syndesmosis between the tibia and fibula.
Ball and socket joints are a type of synovial joint. They permit a wide range of motion but are not held together by short fibers embedded in connective tissues.

questions.explanation: Fibrous joints are characterized by short fibers embedded in connective tissues, which provide minimal movement, making them different from synovial joints that have a joint capsule and allow more extensive movement. Synovial joints include types such as saddle joints and ball and socket joints, which are highly mobile and not held together by short fibers. This structural distinction makes fibrous joints unique in their composition and function.

questions.text: Hip Joint and shoulder joint are the examples of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ball and socket joints, like the hip and shoulder joints, allow for multi-directional movement and rotation. These joints have a spherical head (ball) fitting into a rounded cavity (socket), providing a high degree of freedom.

Hinge joints permit movement in one plane, similar to the motion of a door hinge. Examples include the elbow and knee joints, which allow bending and straightening actions only, not the wide range of motion seen in ball and socket joints.
Fibrous joints are immovable and held together by dense connective tissue. These are found in structures like the sutures of the skull, where no movement is possible.
Cartilaginous joints allow limited movement and are connected by cartilage. Examples include the joints between vertebrae in the spine, which allow for slight flexibility and cushioning.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the 'Ball and Socket Joint.' Both the hip and shoulder joints exemplify this type of synovial joint, characterized by a spherical bone head fitting into a cup-like socket, allowing for a wide range of movement including rotation.The other options are incorrect for the following reasons:- Hinge Joint: This joint allows movement primarily in one direction, such as bending and straightening, and does not provide the extensive range of motion that ball and socket joints do.- Fibrous Joint: These joints do not allow movement, as they are tightly connected by fibers, making them unsuitable examples of joints like the hip and shoulder.- Cartilaginous Joints: These joints offer limited movement and are not as freely movable as ball and socket joints.

questions.text: Proximal end of which bone is involved in formation of hip joint
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The tibia, primarily a part of the lower leg, is not involved in forming the hip joint. It articulates with the femur at the knee, not at the hip.
The fibula is a slender bone located alongside the tibia in the lower leg. It does not participate in forming the hip joint, as it does not connect to the pelvis.
The proximal end of the femur is integral to the hip joint's structure. It fits into the acetabulum of the pelvic bone, creating the ball-and-socket joint of the hip.
The humerus is the upper arm bone and is involved in forming the shoulder joint, not the hip. It does not connect to the pelvis or the lower limbs.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the femur. The proximal end of the femur fits into the acetabulum of the hip bone, thus forming the hip joint, which is a ball-and-socket type joint. The other options, such as the tibia and fibula, are bones of the lower leg and do not connect to the pelvis. The humerus is an upper arm bone and is unrelated to the hip joint.

questions.text: Elbow joint is an example of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Pivot joints allow for rotational movement around a single axis, similar to how a doorknob turns. However, the elbow joint does not permit this type of motion, as it primarily allows bending and straightening.

Hinge joints, like the elbow, provide movement in one plane, allowing for flexion and extension—much like a door hinge that opens and closes. This accurately describes the primary function of the elbow joint.

Ball and socket joints, such as those in the shoulder and hip, allow for a wide range of motion in multiple directions. The elbow, however, is limited to flexion and extension, making this option incorrect.
Gliding joints permit bones to slide past one another in various directions, as seen in the wrist. The elbow does not exhibit this type of movement, as it is confined to bending and straightening.

questions.explanation: The elbow allows only flexion and extension, hence it has a hinge joint.

questions.text: Bones are held together in the joints by
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tendons are strong connective tissues that attach muscles to bones. They do not connect bones to each other.
Ligaments are fibrous connective tissues that connect bones to other bones at joints, providing stability and holding the joint together.
Nerves are responsible for transmitting signals throughout the nervous system and play no role in physically holding bones together in joints.
Smooth muscles are involuntary muscles found in the walls of internal organs. They do not participate in maintaining joint stability by holding bones together.

questions.explanation: Ligaments are the correct answer because they are tough, fibrous connective tissues that specifically connect bones to other bones within a joint, ensuring stability and structural integrity. The other options, such as tendons, nerves, and smooth muscles, do not serve this function. Tendons connect muscles to bones, nerves transmit signals, and smooth muscles are involved in involuntary movements within the body, none of which contribute to directly holding bones together in joints.

questions.text: Which of the following bone have no joints
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The coxal bone, or hip bone, is part of the pelvic girdle and forms joints with the femur and sacrum, enabling movement and support.
The sternum, or breastbone, has articulations with the clavicles and ribs, forming part of the thoracic cage and enabling respiratory movements.
Both the coxal bone and the sternum are involved in forming joints with other bones, contributing to movement and structural integrity.
Both the coxal bone and the sternum are associated with joints. Every bone in the human body has some form of articulation, whether for movement or stability.

questions.explanation: All bones in the human body have some form of joint or articulation with other bones. The coxal bone forms the hip joint with the femur and connects to the sacrum, while the sternum articulates with the clavicles and ribs. These joints are essential for movement, support, and the protection of vital organs. Therefore, the correct answer is 'None of them', as there are no bones without joints in the human body.

questions.text: Cartilagenous joints are found in
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cartilaginous joints are primarily found in the vertebral column. The intervertebral discs are examples of such joints, allowing limited movement and providing cushioning between the vertebrae.
The knee joint is a synovial joint, not a cartilaginous joint. It allows significant movement and is one of the largest and most complex joints in the body.
The elbow joint is a synovial joint, not a cartilaginous joint. It functions as a hinge joint, allowing flexion and extension of the forearm.
The hip joint is a synovial joint, not a cartilaginous joint. It is a ball and socket joint, allowing a wide range of motion and rotation of the leg.

questions.explanation: Cartilaginous joints are found in areas like the vertebral column, where the intervertebral discs act as cartilaginous joints, providing cushioning and allowing limited movement. These joints are not found in the wrist, knee, elbow, or hip, as those are primarily synovial joints, allowing for a wider range of motion.

questions.text: The scientific study of structure and function of joints is called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Orthology is concerned with the study of genes and their evolutionary relationships across species, not related to joints.
Arthrology is the branch of science that focuses on the structure and function of joints, making it the correct answer.
Mycology deals with the study of fungi, which is unrelated to the study of joints.
Atherosclerosis is a cardiovascular condition involving plaque buildup in arteries, not related to joints.

questions.explanation: Arthrology is the scientific study of joints, focusing on their structure, function, and classification. This makes it the correct answer. Orthology, Mycology, and Atherosclerosis are unrelated to the study of joints as they pertain to different fields of study: gene relationships, fungi, and arterial health, respectively.

questions.text: Most mobile and least stable type of joint is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ball and socket joints, like the shoulder and hip joints, provide extensive mobility in multiple directions and planes due to their structure. However, this mobility comes at the cost of stability, making them the least stable compared to other joint types.
Cartilaginous joints allow limited movement and are more stable than ball and socket joints. They are connected by cartilage, which provides some flexibility while maintaining joint stability.
Fibrous joints are designed for stability rather than mobility. They offer little to no movement as they are tightly connected by fibrous connective tissue, making them the most stable joint type.

Pivot joints permit rotational movement around a single axis, such as the rotation of the head. Although they provide more movement than fibrous joints, they are neither as mobile nor as unstable as ball and socket joints.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the Ball and socket joint, which allows for extensive movement in multiple directions, such as in the shoulder and hip. This joint's structure features a ball-shaped end fitting into a cup-like socket, promoting mobility but compromising stability. Other joints, like Cartilaginous and Fibrous joints, prioritize stability over mobility, while Pivot joints allow specific rotational movement with moderate stability, making them less mobile than ball and socket joints.

questions.text: All are types of synovial joints except
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The ball and socket joint is a type of synovial joint. It allows for a wide range of rotational movement and is exemplified by the shoulder and hip joints, where the ball-shaped surface of one bone fits into the cup-like depression of another.

Pivot joints are synovial joints that permit rotational movement around a single axis. An example is the joint between the first and second cervical vertebrae, which allows the head to turn from side to side.
Hinge joints are another type of synovial joint. They allow movement in one plane, similar to the movement of a door hinge. Examples include the elbow and knee joints.

Cartilaginous joints are not synovial joints. They are characterized by bones connected entirely by cartilage, which allows for more limited movement compared to synovial joints. They lack a synovial cavity and a synovial membrane.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Cartilaginous joint. Cartilaginous joints are different from synovial joints as they are connected by cartilage without a synovial cavity, offering limited movement. In contrast, synovial joints, such as ball and socket, pivot, and hinge joints, have a synovial cavity filled with synovial fluid, allowing for a greater range of motion. This is why the other options are incorrect—they describe types of synovial joints, all characterized by their mobility and the presence of a synovial cavity.

questions.text: Hinge joint allows movement in _ directions and _ planes.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is correct. A hinge joint allows movement in 2 directions (flexion and extension) within 1 plane, the sagittal plane.
Incorrect. A hinge joint does not function in 2 planes; it only allows movement in the sagittal plane.
Incorrect. A hinge joint moves in a single plane, not many planes. It is limited to the sagittal plane.
Incorrect. A hinge joint allows movement in only 2 directions (flexion and extension), not many directions.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that a hinge joint allows movement in 2 directions and 1 plane. The directions are flexion and extension, which occur in the sagittal plane. This is similar to how a door hinge operates, allowing the door to swing back and forth within a single plane. Other options suggest movement in multiple planes or additional directions, which do not apply to hinge joints.

questions.text: Muscle is connected to bone by
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tendons are strong, fibrous connective tissues that attach muscles to bones, facilitating movement by transferring the force generated by muscle contraction to the bone.
Ligaments connect bones to other bones at joints, providing stability and support, but they do not connect muscles to bones.
Cartilage provides cushioning and a smooth surface for bones to move against each other in joints, but it does not connect muscles to bones.
Cross bridges are part of the molecular mechanism within muscle fibers that facilitate contraction, not structures that connect muscles to bones.

questions.explanation: Muscles are connected to bones by tendons. Tendons are tough, fibrous connective tissues that allow the muscles to exert force and move the bones. Ligaments, while also fibrous tissues, connect bone to bone and provide joint stability. Cartilage is a cushioning tissue found in joints, and cross bridges are molecular structures involved in muscle contraction, not in connecting muscles to bones.

questions.text: Which of the following muscle straightens the elbow joint
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The brachialis muscle is located in the upper arm and primarily functions to flex the elbow, not straighten it.
The biceps muscle, located in the front of the upper arm, is primarily responsible for elbow flexion, bending the elbow rather than straightening it.
The triceps muscle, located at the back of the upper arm, is responsible for extending the elbow, which straightens the arm.
The brachioradialis muscle is located in the forearm and assists in elbow flexion but does not primarily straighten the elbow.

questions.explanation: The triceps muscle is the primary muscle that straightens the elbow joint. It is located at the back of the upper arm and is responsible for extending the elbow. Other muscles such as the brachialis, biceps, and brachioradialis are involved in flexing the elbow, not extending it.

questions.text: Rheumatoid arthritis is the result of which disorder?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Genetic factors can contribute to the risk of developing rheumatoid arthritis, but it is primarily an autoimmune disorder, not purely genetic.
Correct. Rheumatoid arthritis is an autoimmune disorder where the immune system mistakenly attacks the body's own joint tissues, causing inflammation and damage.
Metabolic disorders are related to the biochemical processes in the body, such as diabetes. Rheumatoid arthritis does not fall into this category.
Only one of the options correctly identifies the nature of rheumatoid arthritis. It is an autoimmune disorder, not a combination of all the listed types.

questions.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis is an autoimmune disorder characterized by the immune system attacking the body's own joint tissues, leading to chronic inflammation and joint damage. While genetic factors may influence susceptibility, it is not classified as a purely genetic disorder. It is not a metabolic disorder, which involves biochemical processes, nor is it a combination of all the disorders mentioned.

questions.text: It is the immobility and fusion of vertebral joints
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sciatica involves nerve pain due to compression or irritation of the sciatic nerve, not the immobility and fusion of vertebral joints. It is characterized by radiating pain along the nerve pathway.
Spondylosis is a degenerative spinal condition that leads to changes in the vertebrae and joints, potentially resulting in immobility and fusion of the vertebral joints.
Tetanus is a bacterial infection causing muscle spasms and stiffness, not related to the fusion or immobility of vertebral joints.
Arthritis involves joint inflammation and can affect the spine, but it does not specifically refer to the immobility and fusion of vertebral joints like spondylosis does.

questions.explanation: The condition described is spondylosis, a degenerative disorder affecting the spine that leads to changes in the vertebrae and joints. This can result in reduced mobility and possible fusion of the vertebral joints. Sciatica, tetanus, and arthritis are different conditions: sciatica affects the sciatic nerve, tetanus is a bacterial infection affecting the nervous system, and arthritis is a general term for joint inflammation, none of which specifically describe the immobility and fusion of vertebral joints.

questions.text: Muscles are the specialized form of tissues having _ origin
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Endoderm is responsible for forming the lining of the digestive tract and internal organs. It is not involved in the development of muscle tissues.
Ectoderm forms the outermost layer of the embryo, leading to the development of the skin and nervous system, but not muscles.
Muscles originate from the mesoderm, which is one of the three primary germ layers in the embryo that gives rise to muscle tissues and other organ systems.
Epidermal refers to the outermost layer of the skin, which arises from the ectoderm, not related to muscle development.

questions.explanation: Muscles are derived from the mesodermal layer during embryonic development, which forms various structures including muscles, bones, and the circulatory system. The endoderm gives rise to internal organs, the ectoderm develops into skin and the nervous system, and epidermal structures are part of the ectoderm, not involved in muscle formation.

questions.text: The study of the muscle is called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mycology refers to the study of fungi, not muscles. It involves understanding fungi's biology, taxonomy, and ecology.
Arthrology is the study of joints, which is unrelated to muscle study.
Myology is the correct term for the study of muscles. It covers muscle structure, function, physiology, and associated diseases.
Dermatology is the study of the skin and its diseases, not muscles.

questions.explanation: Myology is the scientific study of muscles, including their structure, function, and related diseases. The other options are incorrect because they refer to different fields: Mycology is the study of fungi, Arthrology is concerned with joints, and Dermatology focuses on the skin.

questions.text: The muscles that are distributed throughout the body
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Smooth muscles are distributed throughout the body in various organs, blood vessels, and the digestive system. They are involuntary muscles that regulate essential functions like digestion and blood flow.
Cardiac muscles are located solely in the heart. They are responsible for the heart's rhythmic contractions, which circulate blood throughout the body, but they are not present throughout the body.
Skeletal muscles are attached to bones and enable voluntary movements. They are not distributed throughout the body in the same widespread manner as smooth muscles.
This option is incorrect because only smooth muscles are distributed throughout the body, unlike cardiac and skeletal muscles which have specific locations and functions.

questions.explanation: Smooth muscles are the correct answer because they are found throughout the body in structures like blood vessels, organs, and the digestive system. They perform involuntary functions essential to various physiological processes. Cardiac muscles, although crucial, are confined to the heart, and skeletal muscles are attached to bones to facilitate movement, not distributed throughout the entire body.

questions.text: Which of following muscle have multinucleated cells
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Smooth muscles contain single nuclei per cell and are primarily located in the walls of internal organs and blood vessels, functioning in involuntary movements.
Cardiac muscle cells usually have a single nucleus, though some cells may be binucleated. They form the heart's musculature, facilitating involuntary heart contractions.
Skeletal muscles consist of fibers that are multinucleated, resulting from the fusion of myoblasts. They attach to bones and enable voluntary movements.
This option is incorrect because only skeletal muscles have multinucleated cells, unlike smooth and cardiac muscles which typically have single nuclei.

questions.explanation: Skeletal muscles are the only muscle type with multinucleated cells. This characteristic arises from the fusion of multiple myoblasts, allowing these muscles to facilitate voluntary movements. Smooth muscles, found in organs and blood vessels, and cardiac muscles, located in the heart, generally have single nuclei per cell and are responsible for involuntary actions.

questions.text: Which of the following muscles are striated muscles ?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Smooth muscles are not striated. They have a uniform appearance without the banding pattern found in other muscle types.
Cardiac muscles have a striated appearance due to the organized arrangement of actin and myosin filaments, similar to skeletal muscles.
Skeletal muscles are striated, characterized by a pattern of light and dark bands due to the orderly arrangement of actin and myosin filaments.
Both cardiac and skeletal muscles are striated, featuring a banded appearance due to the aligned actin and myosin filaments.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D: Both B and C. Cardiac muscles (Option B) and skeletal muscles (Option C) are both striated muscles, meaning they have a striped appearance under a microscope due to the organized arrangement of actin and myosin filaments. Smooth muscles (Option A) are not striated as they lack this organized pattern, resulting in a smooth appearance.

questions.text: Muscles are originated from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The ectoderm forms the outermost layer of cells in the embryo, leading to the development of the nervous system, skin, and related structures. It does not originate muscles.
The mesoderm is responsible for the formation of muscles, bones, connective tissues, and the circulatory system. It is the correct origin of muscles.
The endoderm forms the innermost layer of cells in the embryo, giving rise to the lining of the digestive and respiratory tracts, not muscles.
The term 'epiderm' is not a recognized germ layer. The epidermis is part of the ectoderm layer and pertains to skin, not muscle development.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the mesoderm, as it is the germ layer from which muscles are derived during embryonic development. The mesoderm also forms bones, connective tissues, and the circulatory system. The ectoderm gives rise to structures such as the nervous system and skin, while the endoderm forms internal linings such as those of the digestive tract. 'Epiderm' is not a relevant embryonic germ layer.

questions.text: Which type of muscles are striated and involuntary muscle
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Vascular smooth muscles are found in the walls of blood vessels. They are non-striated and involuntary, regulating blood vessel diameter and blood flow.
Cardiac muscles are striated and involuntary, found in the heart. They contract automatically to pump blood throughout the body.
Skeletal muscles are striated but voluntary. They are attached to bones and enable movements like walking and lifting.
Gut smooth muscles are non-striated and involuntary, found in the digestive tract, aiding in digestion and movement of food.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is cardiac muscles. They are striated due to the organized arrangement of actin and myosin filaments and are involuntary, contracting automatically to facilitate heartbeats. Vascular smooth and gut smooth muscles are involuntary but non-striated. Skeletal muscles are striated but voluntary, requiring conscious control to function.

questions.text: Which muscle is self-stimulating and not fatigued:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Skeletal muscles are controlled by voluntary neural signals and are prone to fatigue, especially during extended or intense physical activity.
Smooth muscles are involuntary and can be activated by neural or hormonal signals, but they are not self-stimulating and can tire over time.
Cardiac muscles are unique in that they are self-stimulating due to their intrinsic pacemaker cells and are highly resistant to fatigue, allowing continuous heart function.
This option is incorrect because only cardiac muscles (C) are self-stimulating and resistant to fatigue, while smooth muscles are not.

questions.explanation: Cardiac muscles have the unique ability to generate electrical impulses independently, a characteristic known as automaticity. This self-stimulating property ensures that the heart maintains a consistent rhythm without external signals. Additionally, cardiac muscles are highly resistant to fatigue, enabling them to sustain continuous contractions throughout a person's life. On the other hand, skeletal muscles require neural input to contract and can experience fatigue with prolonged use. Smooth muscles, while involuntary, also need external stimuli for activation and can tire over time. Thus, the correct answer is cardiac muscles.

questions.text: The majority of muscle in human body is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Smooth muscles are involuntary muscles found in organs and blood vessels, but they do not make up the majority of muscle tissue in the body.
Skeletal muscles are voluntary muscles attached to bones, comprising approximately 40-50% of total body mass, making them the most abundant type of muscle in the human body.
Cardiac muscles are involuntary muscles found only in the heart, and while vital for circulation, they do not constitute the majority of muscle mass.
Circular muscles, often referring to sphincter muscles, are specialized muscles that control openings in the body, but they do not represent a major muscle group overall.

questions.explanation: Skeletal muscle makes up ~40-50% of body mass, responsible for voluntary movement.

questions.text: Sarcomeres are part of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Myofibrils are the contractile organelles within muscle cells. They contain numerous repeating sarcomeres, which are the fundamental units of muscle contraction.
Thick filaments are components of the sarcomere itself, primarily composed of myosin, and are involved in muscle contraction by interacting with thin filaments.
Thin filaments are also components of the sarcomere. They are primarily composed of actin and function alongside thick filaments during muscle contraction.
Actin molecules form the structural basis of thin filaments within the sarcomere, contributing to muscle contraction through their interaction with myosin.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Myofibrils. Sarcomeres are the basic functional units of muscle contraction, and they are organized in a linear series within myofibrils. Myofibrils are thus composed of repeating sarcomeres. Option B (Thick filaments) and Option C (Thin filaments) refer to the protein structures within a sarcomere, not structures that contain sarcomeres. Option D (Actin molecules) are part of the thin filaments, which are components of the sarcomere, rather than structures that contain sarcomeres.

questions.text: When a Sarcomere contracts, then all of the following occurs except
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: During sarcomere contraction, the I band shortens as the actin filaments slide inward towards the center of the sarcomere.

The A band remains the same length during sarcomere contraction because it contains the entire length of the myosin filaments, which do not change in length. Thus, this option is correct as it is the exception.
As the sarcomere contracts, the Z lines move closer together, marking the shortening of the sarcomere.
The H zone, which consists only of myosin filaments, narrows and eventually disappears as the actin and myosin filaments overlap more during contraction.

questions.explanation: During the contraction of a sarcomere, the I band shortens, the Z lines come closer, and the H zone narrows and can disappear. However, the A band does not shorten; it remains constant in length because it represents the length of the myosin filaments which do not change during contraction. Therefore, the correct answer is that the A band shortens, as it is the exception and does not actually occur during sarcomere contraction.

questions.text: I band contains
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The I band contains only actin filaments, forming the light region of the sarcomere. It is situated between two A bands, where actin and myosin filaments do not overlap.
The I band does not contain myosin filaments. Myosin is primarily located in the A band, not the I band.
This is incorrect because the I band consists solely of actin filaments, with no myosin present.
This is incorrect because the I band contains actin filaments only, without any myosin filaments.

questions.explanation: The I band is a specific region in the sarcomere that contains only actin filaments, giving it a light appearance in contrast to the A band, which contains both actin and myosin filaments. Option A is correct, as it accurately describes the composition of the I band. Options B, C, and D are incorrect because they either include myosin or exclude actin, which does not accurately describe the I band's composition.

questions.text: It is the complex of three polypeptide chain:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Actin is a globular protein that forms the thin filaments in muscle fibers, primarily composed of two intertwined chains, not three.
Myosin is a motor protein that forms the thick filaments in muscle fibers, composed of multiple polypeptide chains, but it is not specifically a complex of three distinct chains.

Troponin is a complex consisting of three specific polypeptide chains: Troponin C, Troponin I, and Troponin T. It is essential for muscle contraction regulation by interacting with calcium ions and tropomyosin.
Tropomyosin is a long, fibrous protein that stabilizes actin filaments. It is composed of two polypeptide chains, not three distinct ones.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Troponin, which is a complex of three distinct polypeptide chains: Troponin C, Troponin I, and Troponin T. This complex is crucial for the regulation of muscle contraction as it responds to calcium ion levels and interacts with tropomyosin to facilitate actin-myosin interactions. The other options are incorrect because:Actin is composed of two chains, not three.Myosin has multiple chains but is not specifically a three-chain complex.Tropomyosin consists of two chains, not three.

questions.text: T tubules is the part of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: T tubules are not part of myofibrils. Myofibrils are the contractile structures within muscle fibers, responsible for muscle contraction, but they do not include the T tubules.
T tubules are extensions of the sarcolemma, which is the cell membrane of muscle cells. They facilitate the transmission of action potentials into the muscle fiber, aiding in muscle contraction.
T tubules are not part of neurons. Neurons are nerve cells that transmit signals in the nervous system, distinct from muscle cell structures.
T tubules are not part of the endoplasmic reticulum. The endoplasmic reticulum is an organelle involved in the synthesis of proteins and lipids, not directly related to the function of T tubules.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that T tubules are part of the sarcolemma. T tubules are invaginations of the sarcolemma and play a critical role in conducting action potentials from the cell surface to the interior of the muscle fiber, ensuring that the muscle contracts in a coordinated manner. The other options are incorrect: Myofibrils are contractile elements, not associated with T tubules directly; Neurons are nerve cells, not muscle components; and the Endoplasmic Reticulum is an organelle involved in cellular synthesis processes, not muscle excitation.

questions.text: The light bands of the skeletal muscle is known as
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The light bands of skeletal muscle are called isotropic bands or I bands. These regions allow light to pass through more easily, giving them a lighter appearance under a microscope.
Anisotropic bands, also known as A bands, are the darker regions of skeletal muscle. They contain both actin and myosin filaments and appear darker because they are denser.
Intercalated discs are specialized structures found in cardiac muscle, not skeletal muscle. They facilitate synchronized contraction of cardiac cells.
Cross bridges are formed during muscle contraction between myosin heads and actin filaments. They are not a structural feature associated with the light bands of skeletal muscle.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is isotropic bands, also known as I bands. These are the light bands in skeletal muscle that appear lighter under a microscope due to their less dense arrangement of actin filaments.
